data.title: FSOL: There Are Better Options For Solana Exposure
data.description:
Back in late-October, I wrote an article for Seeking Alpha that compared the Bitwise Solana ETF (BSOL) and the Grayscale Solana Trust (GSOL), with the latter having been successfully converted from a closed-end product to a spot ETF. In the time since that piece was published, several additional spot Solana (SOL-USD) ETFs have come to market. In this article, we'll look at the Fidelity Solana Fund ETF (FSOL) and see how it stacks up to BSOL and GSOL. In the past, I've been a fan of Fidelity's spot ETF products relative to alternatives. Perhaps surprisingly, FSOL is not one that I see myself buying.

FSOL Details

Like the other funds mentioned, FSOL is a spot Solana ETF that aims to provide investors exposure to Solana in a straightforward traditional financial product.

Fund Name Inception Expense Ratio AUM (millions)
Bitwise Solana Staking ETF 10/27/2025 0.20%* $797.38
Grayscale Solana Staking ETF 11/18/2021 0.35%* $200.42
Fidelity Solana Fund ETF 11/17/2025 0.25%* $160.45

Sources: Seeking Alpha, fund issuers, *active waivers

The fund is competitive with BSOL and GSOL in areas like expense ratio; each of which have waived their fees and currently charge investors 0%. FSOL has quickly been able to scale AUM close to GSOL, but BSOL is far and away the market leader in assets under management. Each of these funds are staking-enabled, but FSOL has the lowest staking ratio of the three.

Staking Ratio Gross Staking Reward Rate
GSOL 100% 7.60%
BSOL 100% 6.74%
FSOL 72% 4.51%

Sources: Grayscale, Bitwise, Fidelity

BSOL and GSOL are both at 100% as of January 14th. FSOL's staking ratio is 72%. Additionally, the gross staking rewards rates between the three funds are all over the place. Bitwise is using an annualized 90-day window of data to derive the BSOL staking reward rate. Grayscale is doing something similar going back to mid-October, and Fidelity is using an annualized 30-day trailing average. Given the fact that FSOL has a smaller staking ratio, the difference in gross staking reward rate makes some sense.

In the company's prospectus, Fidelity does mention that it could stake as much as 100% of the assets in the fund. However, my interpretation of the text is that a full 100% staking ratio with FSOL may not be likely due to the company's desire to have assets available for redemption and/or expenses.

Ultimately, the lower staking ratio and the higher long-term fee are two legitimate reasons that I think passing on FSOL in favor of BSOL is a justifiable decision. But there is actually another factor that I find to be disappointing regarding FSOL, and that's the use of custodians for the fund's staking services. One of the big reasons why I liked Fidelity's spot Bitcoin (BTC-USD) and spot Ethereum (ETH-USD) offerings was because the company holds those assets in self-custody rather than utilizing a third party provider. For me, this minimizes risk, and I'm surprised Fidelity decided to go the other way with FSOL.

But beyond just deciding between ETF products, investors also need to consider the merits of Solana itself before making any decisions about which fund to allocate to. And the latest network data isn't all that great from where I sit.

Solana Network Data

Here's the good news: Solana's network usage continues to be at or near the top of the market in several important categories like raw users, transactions, and fees. The bad news is Solana is experiencing significant quarter-over-quarter and year-over-year declines in each of these metrics.

Network Data Q4-25 Q3-25 Q4-25 YoY QoQ
DAAs (millions) 6.0 3.7 2.5 -58.3% -32.4%
Transactions (billions) 7.5 8.8 6.9 -8.0% -21.6%
Fees (000s) $396.4 $122.8 $65.7 -83.4% -46.5%

Source: Artemis

In Q4-25, daily active addresses fell by 58% from the prior year. Transactions were down by just 8%. But it was fees, down a staggering 83% year over year, that I think could be cause for concern for Solana bulls.

Where the network is beginning to make a splash is in the world of tokenized 'Real World Assets' or RWA. At $1.1 billion in tokenized assets on Solana (not counting stablecoins), Solana is the third most utilized blockchain for RWA after Ethereum and BNB Chain (BNB-USD), and it had the second best positive net flow story over the last month with $323 million coming into the ecosystem:

Tokenized assets on Solana include T-bills, private credit, corporate bonds, and equities. Stablecoin supply - while off highs - is up over 150% since the start of 2025 from $5 billion to slightly above $13 billion:

While stablecoin supply is up over the last year, stablecoin transfer volume last quarter was well off highs:

Stablecoins (trillions) Q4-25 Q3-25 Q4-25 YoY QoQ
Total Market Transfer Vol $10.2 $15.8 $19.7 93.1% 24.7%
Solana Transfer Vol $3.2 $0.9 $1.3 -59.4% 44.4%
Solana Share 31.4% 5.7% 6.6% -79.0% 15.8%

Source: Artemis

Stablecoin transfer volume on Solana fell significantly year over year from $3.2 trillion in Q4-24 to $1.3 trillion in Q4-25. Not only was this a 59% decrease year over year, but it came at a time when the total market for stablecoin transfer volume nearly doubled. As a result, Solana's share of stablecoin transfer volume collapsed from 31% a year ago to just under 7% last quarter.

Risks

This network data should serve as a reminder that despite the positive catalysts for the digital asset ecosystem over the last year or so, utility absolutely does matter. And while I believe Solana is a chain that has proven to be fast, cheap, and market-leading in many ways, there is a lot of competition in this space, and just because an ETF product exists, it doesn't necessarily make it a good investment. That said, I think there are much worse long term investments in the cryptocurrency market than Solana. But as investors, we have to acknowledge when network metrics are improving and when they're doing the opposite. Solana is losing stablecoin transfer share to other networks all while network fees collapse. These are not reasons to be overly bullish, in my opinion.

Closing Takeaways

My view on Solana is mixed. I certainly don't hate the idea of long exposure to Solana today. But I wouldn't get over my skis. I believe the chain is going to compete with larger blue-chip networks like Ethereum in the stablecoin and RWA markets over the long run. Given that, SOL is an exciting asset in a reality where public networks are competing for TradFi asset tokenization. But the declining usage of the network over the last few quarters shouldn't be overlooked.

Regarding ETF products, FSOL will likely serve as a perfectly fine way to express a long-term SOL thesis. If SOL appreciates, FSOL should outperform given the assets underlying the fund are staked for network rewards. Still, I'm choosing BSOL over FSOL for a handful of reasons. First, BSOL is slightly cheaper by long term expense ratio. Second, BSOL has a larger staking ratio. Third, FSOL utilizes custodians, which was a competitive edge that Fidelity had with prior products that it no longer has with FSOL. I'll initiate FSOL with a 'hold' rating at this time.

data.title: CHPY: Massive Performance Since Its Inception
data.description:
YieldMax Semiconductor Portfolio Option Income ETF (CHPY) has had an exceptional performance since its inception in April 2025.

What makes it especially impressive is it couldn't have launched at a less opportunistic time, as it coincided with the implementation of President Trump's tariffs, which had a significant impact on stocks across most categories.

While CHPY did experience a pullback from its opening high of roughly $48.00 per share in early April, to a low of $40.39 on April 21, 2025, it hasn't looked back since, rapidly soaring to about $54.00 per share on May 13, 2025, and trading sideways for a while, before jumping to a 52-week high of $60.30.

Unsurprisingly, the ETF has experienced some volatility in price, but its overall trajectory has been consistently moving up. It has also managed to significantly outperform the S&P 500 by a growth of 31% compared to S&P growth of 19.55%.

In this article, we'll look at the performance of CHPY and whether or not it can continue to generate sustainable growth.

The underlying

My outlook for covered call ETFs like CHPY is you have to focus primarily on the underlying stock or basket of stocks in order to determine their potential.

In the case of CHPY, the underlying is primarily in the semiconductor sector, which explains CHPY's success since it launched, reflecting the strong demand for chips during that time, which should continue on for the foreseeable future.

The top holdings at the time of this writing include Broadcom at 11.32%; NVIDIA at 9.79%; Micron at 9.02%, and Lam Research at 8.35%, accounting for well over 33% of the underlying.

With those and similar stocks as part of the portfolio options being written against, I think CHPY has more than enough runway for growth over the next year or so.

Some money may be rotating out of chip suppliers to the energy sector, which in the near term probably has the most potential to utilize AI to increase productivity, reduce costs, and widen margins, but there is still plenty of demand for chips as the market stands today.

For that reason, even with the boost in share price since its inception, I think CHPY has room for plenty of more growth, and more importantly, room to continue to pay out a hefty distribution.

Future catalysts for the semiconductor sector

Several macro forces are probably going to continue to shape CHPY’s future, including the AI compute super-cycle; adaptation from tariffs and onshoring; the next wave of edge AI, energy, and industrial automation; and ongoing cyclicality.

1. The AI compute super‑cycle

Demand for accelerators, GPUs, and high‑bandwidth memory will without a doubt continue to exceed supply. Broadcom, NVIDIA, and Micron are direct beneficiaries. As long as these types of companies and enterprises continue to expand AI infrastructure, CHPY’s underlying basket remains strongly supported.

2. Onshoring and tariff‑driven adaptation

The tariff environment that, at the beginning, created volatility is now ramping up domestic semiconductor investment. CHPY’s holdings—especially Broadcom and Lam Research—stand to benefit from increased U.S. manufacturing and equipment spending.

3. Energy, industrial automation, and edge AI

While some capital is rotating into the energy sector due to AI‑driven gains in efficiency, this shift still reinforces semiconductor demand. AI adoption in energy, logistics, and manufacturing requires more chips, not fewer of them.

4. Cyclicality remains important

Semiconductors remain cyclical. Hyperscale, memory pricing, wafer fab utilization, and capex cycles can result in volatility. But the long‑term upward trajectory remains in place.

Taking into account these catalysts, CHPY’s underlying portfolio appears to have meaningful runway through at least 2026.

Distributions

Distributions for CHPY have remained consistent and strong since the ETF was launched, with the lowest weekly payout being $0.3454, paid out on 4-25-2025, and the highest being $62.29, paid out on 5-2-2025. The general range of distributions paid is from the mid-$30s to the mid-$40s. The latest was the most recent payout of $0.5040 on 1-8-2026.

Looking ahead, I don't see anything over the next year that would interfere with the ongoing distribution price range of CHPY, unless there is a significant correction in the chip market from a demand perspective.

If there is a temporary correction from investors taking profits in the chip sector, that will only result in a temporary blip on the performance of CHPY, and it should rebound as it has in the past.

Risks

The major question for the sustainable performance of CHPY is how long the chip sector will continue to experience strong demand for its products. As mentioned above, there has been some rotation to the energy sector because of the potential positive impact coming from the AI sector, which relies on chips, but that doesn't mean demand for chips is declining, only that the application of AI to various aspects of business in general, and the energy sector in particular, could result in some downward pressure if there are outflows in some of the underlying basket of companies CHPY is focusing on.

Consequently, if there is a slightly moderate pullback of the underlying, it could result in CHPY trading sideways or slightly down, with distributions potentially dropping into the $30s. I don't think that will happen in the short term, but it is a possibility if investors believe we're close to entering into an AI bubble.

If that happens, I think it's more likely to be in the latter part of 2026 or 2027, if it does happen during that time period.

Another risk is how high CHPY has climbed, with it trading at about $60.00 per share at the time of this writing. If shareholders are spooked because of bubble fears, it would take a hit from the fear trade.

The good news there is, based upon the short historical performance of CHPY, it seems to have some resilience in negative market conditions, such as the tariff effect. That said, we have yet to see how the ETF will respond to actual market conditions that aren't as favorable as they are during this particular period.

If there is a surprise to the downside in the near term, the share price and distribution will suffer some erosion, although it may not be NAV erosion, depending on the negative catalyst that caused the pullback under that scenario.

Assuming a long downturn, those that are in at a higher cost basis will be challenged as to the timeframe required to generate positive, total returns.

Conclusion

CHPY is one of the better performing covered call ETFs in existence. It has shown the ability to not only bounce back from price erosion not associated with NAV erosion from paying out a distribution that isn't covered by the premium income generated from options.

Based upon how one views the semiconductor sector at this time, even at the higher price level it is trading at now, it still appears to have a strong chance to continue to perform strongly. For me, I would like to see a general correction in price to get in at a more favorable price point. As the semiconductor market stands today, I think any correction coming from taking profits or something in the new cycle that doesn't have a foundational impact on the industry, should result in the ETF being able to bounce again, as it has in the past.

On the other hand, if and when demand for chips starts to slow down, CHPY will find itself in the first, potentially long-term crisis since it was launched.

For those that got in early and have reaped the rewards of growth and income since its inception, this probably won't be a big deal because it's probable by the time that happens, they'll be playing with house money.

Since chip demand, even when it slows down, is going to remain in effect for a long time, even a significant downturn should only be temporary. Again, the only concern would be if the distribution being paid out isn't covered by the revenue generated from option premiums. In that case it would be actual NAV erosion, which would result in the ETF having to cut distributions to mitigate the challenges associated with maintaining NAV.

I don't think this is going to happen this year, and maybe not even the next. But eventually chip demand will slow down, and CHPY growth and income are likely to drop in response to that.

For now, though, it looks to me like it should experience ongoing outperformance, with the caveat it isn't likely to generate the results since April 2025 in the next year.

data.title: Calumet: Specialty Products' Businesses Coming Together Financially
data.description:
Calumet Specialty Products (CLMT) businesses are coming together like the sweetness of a child in one's arms. We recently wrote an article on Cirrus Logic (CRUS) depicting vision investing. With Calumet, the vision is rapidly evolving into execution, a different experience. A vision set in place by Stephen Mawer, then CEO, with an emphasis on renewable energy, plus excellence within the balance of the older business, moves forward. Over the next twelve months businesses are simultaneously aligning into a seamless, positive performance. This process was longer than our original guesstimate, much more. Bruce Fleming, Executive Vice President of Montana Renewables & Corporate Development, noted in a recent fireside chat, that the actions from the prior Federal Administration left critical elements for renewable products (RVO) significantly low. This action drove prices to unprecedented and predictable lows. With Washington's leadership increasing RVOs and management's diligent actions, joyful fruits seem more than likely on the horizon. It's now about execution.

This continues our coverage of Calumet, the last, Calumet: Short Are at Risk, discussing business conditions mainly in the 3rd quarter.

Markets & Businesses

Calumet's make-up includes three types, specialty, traditional refining, and renewable fuels. The specialty portion provides a stable base while fuels deliver variable but positive upsides. Management noted at the last conference call regarding specialty,

"Despite softness reported across much of the broader specialty chemicals world over the past year, our commercial team again sold over 20,000 barrels a day at margins well above $60 per barrel, while also rebuilding some inventory following the Freeport turnaround."

With regard to fuels, management stated,

We also saw strong fuel performance on both the margin and volume front. This reinforces the core advantage of Calumet's integrated model. .

With respect to Performance Brands, the company continues to see growth year over year despite selling a small industrial portion for $100 million earlier in the year. TRUFUEL now benefits from selling space of over 4000 Walmart stores.

The balance of the specialty and tradition refining is summarized in the next slide.

On the left of the slide, the company shows sales volume for both. The gray line is traditional refining. Notice the continued strength above 40,000 barrels per day. The blue line is specialty with a notable slight uptrend. The need for U.S. refining products remains strong, and with continued California and other refinery shutdowns. The spreads likely continue elevated. Over the past several years, significant capital was spent on improving operations plus operating practices. The results show a year-over-year savings at over $70 million. Some of it is in transportation. The margins for specialty remain seasonably strong even with business being mid-cycle.

MRL, the business arm for renewables, continues to experience short-term weakness, particularly with renewable diesel margins. The previous administration left RVO, production targets, low, while continuing to invite foreign dumping. Also, the 3rd quarter experienced "feedstock physical basis widened out, which means feedstocks were about $0.20 a gallon more expensive than the traditional CBOT marker would suggest across the industry." Since the September report, CBOT margins returned to more normal levels. Margins had been crushed. Yet, this business continues as the U.S. and likely world flagship lowest cost producer going forward. Notice the crashing cost of manufacturing created over the past few years in the following slide.

Much of the lower cost came from better management of wastewater. A significant element with MRL is location of cost effective and a variety of feed materials. This coupled, with a two week or less feed chain allows for optimal production execution. This was not an accident; it was designed in from the beginning. The $0.52 breakeven for MRL is industry leading. Also, both the traditional and renewable businesses remained combined for financial reporting. We expect this to change rather quickly in the 1st half of 2026.

An interesting comment on renewables seems more than in order:

Like we mentioned last quarter, our offtake is shaping up to be a diversified slate of direct physical customers, airlines, FBOs and Scope 3 customers of varying sizes, some of which are large multinationals who you might routinely envision when you think of carbon reduction initiatives and some of which are more boutique customers. In many ways, the SAF business highly resembles our Specialty Products business, where the ability to be flexible on logistics, go-to-market in varying ways to suit a wide range of customer needs and sell in all types of sizes make us preferred and differentiated supplier.

Long-term this has significance and illustrates a massive advantage in marketing and other important resource skills.

With respect to the Montana traditional business, it continues with a level of strength. For September, management noted,

On the Montana asphalt side, the third quarter is typically a good one. This quarter, in particular, we saw one of the strongest quarters in recent memory and a $14 million year-over-year gain. Our polymer modified asphalt business continues to be an advantage as well as the niche fuels distribution and with costs dramatically improved, we are pleased to see the impact on the bottom line this quarter.

Notice the asphalt positioning includes a meaningful lean toward specialty.

Depressed Renewable Market

Continuing, during the last conference, management noted the biomass-based diesel production "remains cut back at roughly 60% utilization." Additional color includes:

  • 2025 production seems now stable at 350 million gallons per month.
    • 4.5 billion gallons on a yearly basis.
  • One billion short of the 2025 targets.
  • D4 RIN purchases in the open market will eventually be required to cover this shortfall.
  • Shutdowns of high-cost producers continued.

2025 Update

On January 5th, management released a general business update in a press release. In bullet form, a summary follows:

  • EBITDA between $285 million - $305 million.
    • Past updates signaled close to or at the higher end of guidance, meaning $300 million plus is the likely result.
      • The EBITDA reported for the first three quarters equaled $223 million.
    • 4th Quarter EBITDA likely equals approximately $80 million, an approximate increase of $25 million year over year.
      • Also, 1st quarter last year equaled $55 or approximately the same as 4th quarter the prior year.

Shortly after the update, the company issued new bonds, extinguishing both the 2026 and 2027 issues. The issue placed $405 million at 9.75% due in 2031. The total 26s and 27s equaled $450 million. Management likely used $25 million of the $166 million cash on hand to finish the payoff. In all, interest expenses are lower by approximately $1 million per quarter.

Debt Structure

Of most concern for investors, it's the debt structure. Currently, the total debt equals $2.3 billion, of which $800 million is a DOE loan at 3-4% interest rates. The company has an approximately five-year period interest free. The latest capital structure is shown in the next slide.

The most recent capital changes minus the DOE loan is about $1.3 billion, net cash, or about $500 million above the company target of $800 million.

On the leverage front, a slide from the Goldman presentation reports a history.

Leverage is dropping but not fast enough for some investors.

We finished this; this is where the company stands today. Time to move forward with the future.

Come Together

The past is always important but often doesn't define the future. The company generated $300 million in EBITDA in 2025, a tall and important feat. Now it is time to discuss 2026 and beyond.

SAF margins and production rates define the major financial change. Management provides investors a five-year view.

We've discussed the chart on this slide before, which suggests that global supply and demand is largely balanced in 2025, and that turns to a supply deficit in 2026 as that gap grows each year as European mandates and other global mandates step up. Interestingly, early on, we received questions around this outlook, which really fit into 3 general categories. One, was will Europe really increase their volume mandate. Two, will voluntary demand grow; and 3, weren't we underestimating new supply.

The SAF slide eluted in the discussion follows.

Demand is expected to ramp slowly through 2029 with major demand increases in 2030. With this in mind, management noted European SAF prices have been on the rise, the region that continues mandatory usage, with prices up to $8 per gallon.

The Calumet approach sells via rail using the high-line to the West Coast, Midwest and Canada at an undisclosed premium to renewable diesel but is between $1 - $2 per gallon. For estimating purposes, we have used $1.5. Any of the listed markets could soak up the entire MRL production. Management plans an optimal approach, which now includes trucks into small regional airports.

In early April, a project at Great Falls comes online for an additional 100 million gallons. In the 2028 timeframe, a second SAF expansion startup is planned. Again, a slide shows the projected path.

The 300 million gallons project also increases the total output of MRL from the 12K barrels per day to 16K barrels per day.

So, what this mean? A table follows, illustrating the magnitude of the effect.

EBITDA Change from SAF Added Gallons SAF Net EBITDA Adder * Renewable Margin Change ** ***

Renewable Added EBITDA

 Total
April 2026 100 M $150 M $0.20 - $0.40 (Ave. between $0.8 & $1.00) $55 M **** Approximately $200 M Yearly Run Rate. @
2028 250 M $375 M $0.80 - $1.0 $200 M ***** Approximately $575 M Yearly Run Rate @

* $1.5 per gallon adder.

** Adds to SAF.

*** DGD indicators average 0.60 for all of 2025.

**** 12K barrels per day for 350 days.

***** 16K barrels per day for 350 days.

@ We included yearly run rates in the table. Markets react to future possibilities and will likely value the entity on the run rate, not the actual yearly results.

With respect to the older businesses, management continues to forecast low-hanging fruit ranging in the 20% or so yearly return. Also, last year's 4th quarter report showed significant EBITDA growth year over year, $25 million. With 1st quarter generally similar to the 4th, an expectation of a $25 - $30 increase in EBITDA could be real.

In summary for 2026, the $300 million EBITDA results for 2025 could grow to $525 million to $550 million, almost doubling from 2025. For 2028, EBITDA could reach $900 million. The company's outstanding stock is about 85 million shares. We left out low-hanging specialty fruit.

Management continues beating the drum about asset sales. Products not covered with vertical integration could be sold. We aren't exactly sure what this means or which business, but this fact suggests positive surprises should be expected.

Risk

In our view, RINS, the amounts still in existence, might cause future issues. Thus far, the EPA hasn't given Calumet a full small refinery exception. The most recent report still shows $150 million in possible RIN exposure. Although Specialty has never paid cash for RINS, it must be considered until the courts once again rule in Calumet's favor. We believe that they will, but until it does, a watchful eye is recommended. Another risk, the economy could falter from lowering, in particular, fuel demand and crack spreads. But a possible economic recession seems remote to us.

Summary

This article sets in place markers for future financial results, results with a level of credibility. An almost doubling of the EBITDA, year over year, seems likely for 2026. It's transformative. In two more years, a triple might be in order. With approximately 85 million shares outstanding, $6 - $11 per share earnings is our estimate ($550 million divided by 85 million and $900 divided by 85 million). We rate Calumet a buy going forward with prices under $20. It's cheap. This year seems to be the transition year.

data.title: Atlas Copco: Path To Earnings Growth Ahead Is Visible
data.description:
Investment action

My view for Atlas Copco (ATLKY) is a buy rating because it is set to benefit from a structural tailwind due to demand from AI, electrification, and smart manufacturing. It also has a service business that adds a defensive element to the fundamentals.

Company description

ATLKY is an industrial equipment company that operates four segments: Compressor, Vacuum, Industrial, and Power. It sells mission-critical equipment used in semiconductor factories and generates long-term, high-margin revenue from service and spare parts once that equipment is installed at customer sites.

The Semiconductor Catalyst and Vacuum Recovery

For the past two years, the vacuum segment has been a drag on revenue growth, and the reason was simple: it's because chipmakers were sitting on excess inventory. The vicious cycle here is that when inventories are high, manufacturers delay building new semiconductor factories and upgrading equipment, which directly reduces demand for vacuum systems used in chip production.

I think that headwind is now fading. Demand for High Bandwidth Memory [HBM] is accelerating, and this changes the dynamic entirely. In my view, this shift is likely to be one that is structural rather than cyclical because AI workloads require far more memory than traditional computing, and this demand should continue to grow exponentially regardless of economic cycles. As HBM demand increases, what happens is that existing memory capacity becomes insufficient, and this is why memory spot prices are up significantly in recent history, reflecting a tight supply environment. With chip prices up sharply, manufacturers are incentivized to invest in new capacity. This could be via expanding existing semiconductor factories (brownfield) or building new ones (greenfield). Since advanced chips cannot be made without ultra-high vacuum environments, every new semiconductor factory or capacity upgrade would naturally demand more vacuum pumps and related systems, and that directly benefits ATLKY (higher semiconductor capex flows into ATLKY's Vacuum segment). The impact on the segment EBIT should be even more apparent given the high operating leverage, as most of the costs are fixed.

Industrial Revolution

Aside from the vacuum segment, I think the industrial technique segment has a positive growth angle as well. The worry that most investors have is that a majority of this business is linked to the automotive category, and flat global vehicle production would mean poor demand. In my view, this is not entirely true. I believe there is a secular trend where manufacturing is shifting toward "smart" factories. Today, more than half of industrial tools (per the 2025 capital markets day) are still pneumatic, which means they are powered by compressed air and offer limited precision or data (i.e., not efficient enough). ATLKY helps factory asset owners to upgrade by offering connected, electric tools with precise control and data tracking.

I should be clear that this upgrade is really a "necessity" rather than a "good-to-have" because such tools are essential for electric vehicle battery assembly, where safety and traceability are mandatory. For example, in an EV battery plant, traditional pneumatic tools are used to tighten bolts but cannot record whether each bolt was tightened correctly. ATLKY's connected electric tools not only apply precise torque (rotational force used to tighten or loosen a bolt) but also record and store the data for every fastening step.

Importantly, for ATLKY, replacing a pneumatic tool with a smart tool can lift revenue per unit by 2x to 5x (per the capital markets day), and this helps ATLKY to decouple from car production volume. Also, since there is software embedded in these tools, they require ongoing service and support, which helps ATLKY to build stickier relationships.

Services revenue

Finally, I believe there is a piece of ATLKY business that deserves to be a much higher key part of the bull case. Specifically, I am referring to the Services segment, which makes up ~40% of revenue but is ~60% mix of EBIT. Let me give a sense of how sticky this service revenue is. In ATLKY's largest segment, compressor technique, service revenue has never declined organically over the past 18 years, including through the Global Financial Crisis and the covid pandemic. I believe the logic is simple and straightforward. While equipment orders may slow during down cycles, factories still need compressors to operate, and those systems require regular maintenance. Unless we are talking about a complete shutdown/bankruptcy, those factories still need to operate, and that gives ATLKY a recurring stream of cash flow. Aside from stability, the service business is also important because it lowers the risk of margin collapse. Since service revenues are stable and can cover a large portion of fixed costs, EBIT margins tend to be less volatile even in downturns. For shareholders, this helps to prevent the sharp earnings collapse.

Q4 2025 Earnings Preview

My focus will be on the near term outlook. Specifically, I like to hear management become more confident in hinting at sequential improvement in growth. This would send the strongest signal that ATLKY has indeed turned the corner. It would be best if we see an uptick in vacuum order intake, as I take that as the strongest sign that a new capex cycle is forming. I would also pay attention to Industrial segment margins. If they hold despite weak auto volumes, it confirms that the pivot toward smart factories is improving margins structurally

Valuation

I believe Consensus has done a good job in modeling out the business. My focus is really on the Vacuum and Industrial segment here, and I'm assuming Compressor and Power just grow at a mid-single-digit (GDP+x% pace). For vacuum, this should be the fastest-growing segment given the underlying demand led by AI (which requires a significant amount of semiconductor chips). The industrial segment should see growth turn positive as the world pivots more towards smart factories, making ATLKY less reliant on automotive production cycles. Together, revenue should easily grow at mid-to-high single digits for the foreseeable future. And like I said, there is a lot of fixed cost in this business, so margin should expand as revenue scales, which implies EPS to grow faster than the top line (more towards high single digits). That should bring us to ATLKY delivering ~SEK7.30 adj. EPS in FY28. Using historical precedent, with a high-single-digit adj. EPS growth profile, ATLKY should trade closer to ~28x NTM PE, which translates to a share price of SEK204.4, or ~$22.

Risk

The risk that investors need to undertake is timing. The recovery in the vacuum segment depends on chipmakers increasing capital spending. If AI demand slows or semiconductor factory investments are delayed, the rebound could take longer than expected. Also, a broader industrial slowdown or weaker service pricing could pressure margins.

Conclusion

I give ATLKY a buy rating. The recovery in the vacuum segment provides a clear earnings catalyst, while the industrial technique upgrade cycle and the service business model add layers to the bull case. This combination should allow earnings to compound through economic cycles, and with operating leverage kicking in, adj. EPS should easily grow in high single digits.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Lionsgate: Sell This Rally On Rising Debt (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
In my view, 2026 will be the year for small-cap stocks to shine. 2025's market gains were almost entirely driven by large-cap tech stocks, but with valuation multiples now stretched to uncomfortable levels, I'm looking for small and mid-cap stocks, especially in unloved sectors, to outperform their larger peers this year.

Stock selection is critical in this space, however, and we have to carefully monitor fundamental performance. Lionsgate (LION), the Hollywood studio company, is looking increasingly frail as its revenue dries up, and it relies on a future pipeline to drive growth. Still, investors have piled into the stock recently, sending it up ~30% since its last earnings release in November.

I last wrote an article upgrading Lionsgate to a neutral rating in September when the stock was trading at $7 per share. At $9 per share, however, my tune definitely changes. Lionsgate's growth trends worsened sharply in its most recent quarter as recent content dried up, and the company positions itself for a 2026 slate to drive growth: requiring us to have more faith in the company's ability to turn itself around. That said, amid the higher valuation, which is also stacked on top of higher debt, I encourage locking in any gains on this name and am downgrading it to sell.

Sharp contraction in revenue

Let's first discuss the fundamental deterioration in the business. The chart below showcases Lionsgate's trended revenue in each of its business lines:

In fiscal Q2 (the September quarter), Lionsgate reported a -42% y/y drop in revenue. Sales of films (motion picture segment) fell -33% y/y to $276 million, while revenue from TV fell -52% y/y to $199 million. Consolidated revenue of $475 million sharply missed Wall Street's expectations of $541 million, which would have implied a much more benign -24% y/y drop.

Of course, we have to expect that Lionsgate's revenue will be lumpy, especially in the motion picture business. The company noted that it faced a tough comp versus 5 wide-release films in the second quarter of last year. We do note that the film business was slightly more profitable this year, generating $31 million of segment profit versus essentially breakeven last year, as the prior year was burdened by the financial underperformance of Borderlands (starring Cate Blanchett and released in August 2024; based on the popular video game franchise by Take-Two Interactive Software (TTWO)).

It's in the TV business, however, that I'm more disappointed. Back in Q1, the TV segment had showcased 20% y/y growth. The company chalked up the revenue declines (and the miss to Street expectations) to the timing of episode delivery. Still, if we now compare 1H'26 to 1H'25, two-quarter revenue of $487 million still declined -26% y/y. The company notes that it has far more scripted deliveries planned next year, which means we may have to wait for FY27 for a recovery.

One of the core areas that I had previously thought to be attractive for Lionsgate is its back library of TV series. The company highlighted that TTM library revenue reached $1 billion in cumulative sales for the first time: but clearly, more active selling of back content isn't helping to push the TV studio into growth territory.

Management is essentially asking investors to have confidence in the company's upcoming slate. In movies, the company is looking ahead to the release of The Housemaid (released in December, starring Sydney Sweeney) and the latest installment of Now You See Me (released November). Looking further out, the second Hunger Games prequel, Sunrise on the Reaping, releases in November 2026 (this is arguably Lionsgate's biggest franchise). In TV, the company secured renewals for hits including The Studio, The Rainmaker, and The Hunting Wives.

We note that on the Q&A portion of the Q2 earnings call, Wall Street analysts drilled into more specifics about what was driving management's confidence in the company's slate. CFO Adam Fogelson waffled a bit on the question, citing social media views but then ultimately noting that its ability to predict performance won't be perfect. Per his remarks on the call:

I'll start with your confidence question in terms of the slate. Clearly, there is no perfect predictor out there. But that having been said, we are seeing a ton of excitement. Just yesterday, the day before, Exhibition was talking about a number of our films in the fourth quarter and into next year that they're seeing a lot of enthusiasm. We're getting a lot of traction online. I can't either confirm or deny that we participated in the Heist, but Now You See Me has been all over social media, and The Housemaid is generating a ton of conversation. The Michael trailer generated over 30 million views in the first 6 hours of its launch today, which is 50% more than what John Wick 4 trailer delivered in its first 6 hours. The conversations around the Hunger Games with every announcement of casting and the massive global demand for Resurrection. All of that gives us confidence.

We recognize that you can't be perfect in predicting each film, but when you look at the portfolio and the films I mentioned and a lot of the mid and small budget films that we have on our slate, it does give us an exceptional amount of confidence. And the growth we're talking about is not based on projecting that every one of those turns into a blockbuster, but we think we've got a bunch in that mix."

The point to illustrate here is that Lionsgate is banking almost entirely on hopes for its upcoming slate: and especially in a tougher economy, this is far from a certainty.

Rising leverage

The other trend we have to keep an eye on is Lionsgate's burgeoning leverage. Net corporate debt rose by ~$0.2 billion sequentially to $1.73 billion. Meanwhile, leverage against the company's trailing OIBDA (an industry measure that is effectively similar to adjusted EBITDA) rose to 6.5x.

As a reminder, commercial banks typically consider ~3x to be the upper end of the threshold for what constitutes a highly leveraged company.

We do note that in September, Lionsgate cut ~5% of its staff (about 50 heads), which results in a cumulative total of ~20% of its staff cut over the past 18 months. While these are deep cuts, we don't think it's nearly enough to combat the company's sharp revenue declines and rising leverage profile.

Key takeaways

In my view, Lionsgate's recent rebound rally has little backing in fundamentals. I'm wary about the company's reliance on its future slate to drive growth while current trends appear weak, and investors should be sensitive to the company's rising debt burden as well. It's a good opportunity to sell this rally: steer clear and invest elsewhere.

data.title: Commodities: Iranian Supply Risks Ease, But Have Not Disappeared
data.description:
By Warren Patterson, Head of Commodities Strategy and Ewa Manthey, Commodities Strategist

Oil prices came under significant pressure yesterday, with concerns easing over immediate US action against Iran.

Energy: Brent flat price weakens while timespreads hold firmer

After five days of gains, oil prices came under significant pressure yesterday, with ICE Brent settling 4.15% lower. The sell-off came as the US avoided taking immediate action against Iran amid ongoing protests in the country.

There had been growing noise in recent days that the Trump administration was considering military intervention, raising concerns not only over Iranian supply, but broader risks to supply from the Persian Gulf.

Any escalation with Iran will also raise concerns about potential disruption to oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz, a chokepoint where around 20m b/d passes.

While risks have eased somewhat, they remain significant, keeping the market nervous in the short term. However, the longer this goes on without any US intervention, the risk premium will continue to fade, allowing more bearish fundamentals to dominate.

While we maintain a bearish outlook for the market, we are seeing strength in the prompt ICE Brent timespread. The spread held up relatively well yesterday despite weakness in the flat price. This suggests some tightness in the spot market, likely due to a decline in Kazakh oil flows from the CPC terminal.

The European gas market continued to rally yesterday. TTF settled more than 4.2% higher on the day, taking the market back above EUR33/MWh. The move was driven by another cold snap forecast for large parts of Europe towards the end of this month.

Meanwhile, EU gas storage remains tighter than usual, with it less than 52% full at the moment vs. a 5-year average of 67% full. We have been warning about the potential for a short-covering rally in the market, given the sizeable short position that funds held in TTF through the early part of winter. Investment funds have already reduced their net short in TTF from 92.76TWh in mid-December to 55.14TWh currently.

Agriculture: Cocoa prices decline on weaker grindings

Cocoa prices came under further pressure yesterday, with London cocoa settling 2.3% lower on the day. This follows weaker-than-expected grinding data from Europe. Data from the European Cocoa Association shows that cocoa grindings fell 8.3% year on year to 304.5mt in 4Q25, the lowest for this period since 2013. This leaves total grindings for 2025 down 6.1% YoY.

National Federation of Cooperative Sugar Factories Ltd. data shows that sugar production in India jumped 21% YoY to 15.9mt between 1 Oct 2025 and 15 Jan 2026. Factories crushed 176.4mt of cane over the period compared to 148.4mt a year ago.

The average sugar recovery from cane was 9% vs. 8.8% in the last season. Meanwhile, around 519 mills were crushing as of 15 January, slightly up from 514 mills last year. The group reiterated its 2025/26 total sugar production estimate of 31.5mt, accounting for volumes diverted to ethanol.

In its monthly update, the International Grains Council (IGC) increased its 2025/26 global wheat output forecasts from 830mt to 842mt. Consumption projections increased to 823mt from previous estimates of 819mt.

As a result, global wheat ending stock estimates increased from 275mt to 283mt. For soybeans, ending stock estimates remain unchanged at 77mt. Finally, for corn, the council increased its global ending stocks estimate from 300mt to 305mt on stronger production forecasts.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: KRE: Stabilization With A CRE Overhang
data.description:
Introduction & Thesis

There are many ways to gain exposure to different parts of the US banking sector. One of the best known is indexing using the State Street SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF (KRE). This gives you exposure to the regional banking sector, leaving aside the world's big banks, like The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS), JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM), Citigroup Inc. (C), and Bank of America (BAC), making it easier to bet on a sector where the risk of three or four names dominating all returns is reduced. The ETF seeks capital appreciation by tracking the S&P Regional Banks Select Industry benchmark index. This index in turn invests in shares of US regional banks, limiting the maximum weight per asset within the index to 4.5%.

As we will see below, the American banking sector, and more specifically the regional subsector, is clearly returning to normal in terms of its industry, coming out of the tense environment that arose with the bankruptcy of SVB and Singular Bank in 2023. For the purposes of this analysis, I recommend holding the vehicle if you already have it in your portfolio.

Today, the main risk in the banking sector is concentrated in the Commercial Real Estate (CRE) business line. These are loans and bank exposures linked to non-residential real estate, which are directly influenced by interest rates, which were much lower when these transactions were opened (rates at 0%). Today, with higher interest rates (3.5%), these loans with refinancing at maturity will increase due to rate hikes. For this reason, I recommend holding the ETF for the time being. In the sector analysis below, we will see how this is the main risk.

ETF Overview

The main characteristics of this ETF indicate the type of vehicle we are working with. With a 30-day yield of 2.25%, a distribution yield of 2.40%, and an index dividend yield of 2.68%, we have the complete flow of what the fund generates in terms of income. After deducting expenses and net commissions, the fund generates a 2.25% return on invested capital. With an expense ratio of 0.35%, part of the index dividend yield is lost along the way, and what ultimately reaches the investor is a lower figure (2.25%). We can also see that the fund currently has 146 securities in its portfolio, with an approximate EPS growth of 14.55% over 3 to 5 years, which is not insignificant.

Fund Performance

When looking at the ETF's returns relative to its benchmark, we see that the benchmark consistently outperforms the fund (across all time periods). This is due to the passive management applied by the ETF. By replicating the index itself using resampling methods and charging fees, the ETF achieves a slightly worse replication in terms of performance. Over the last 10 years, the after-tax return has been 5.51% per annum for the ETF, compared to 7.43% for the index. This difference of almost two percentage points is the impact of the fees mentioned in the previous paragraph.

The Holdings

The portfolio of this ETF is very easy to understand. As we have mentioned, it replicates its benchmark index, so it is fully exposed to the US regional banking sector. And, like the index, it's equal-weighted. The ETF's top 10 holdings include names such as Cullen/Frost Bankers Inc. (CFR) at 2.00%, Popular, Inc. (BPOP) at 1.99%, Regions Financial Corporation (RF) at 1.94%, SouthState Bank Corporation (SSB) at 1.94%, Hancock Whitney Corporation (HWC) at 1.94%, M&T Bank Corporation (MTB) at 1.94%, Citizens Financial Group, Inc. (CFG) at 1.94%, Wintrust Financial Corporation (WTFC) at 1.93%, UMB Financial Corporation (UMBF) at 1.93%, and Western Alliance Bancorporation (WAL) at 1.92%. What interests me most about this is not only “which banks” it has, but how they are weighted. The table above shows that even the largest positions weigh around 2%, and the top 10 are very poorly concentrated. This reduces the probability of losses due to idiosyncratic risk, as the portfolio is highly diversified.

Banking Sector Statistics - FDIC

Below we analyze the different metrics corresponding to the regional banking sector from the latest FDIC report. With regard to aggregate income (quarterly income), we can see that it has remained surprisingly strong after the crash that occurred in 2023 with the failure of two regional banks. This implies that the aggregate profitability of the FDIC-insured banking system is capable, at the net income level, of absorbing losses, should they occur. On the other hand, we see how the NIM (Net Interest Margin) has recovered since 2023, due to the high interest rate policy mentioned in the first section. Here, the main question will be whether the cost of funding (deposits) puts greater pressure on income, reducing this net interest margin. If this were to happen, it would be the main catalyst for changing the rating of the article from hold to sell.

The second set shows the metrics for the change in quarterly credit loss provisions and the change in loan balances. In the first, we can see that there is currently no change in the aggregate data, a situation that supports our hold approach. In terms of the change in loan balances, we see that credit growth has moderated compared to past expansionary phases, a sign of a more prudent system following the shock of 2023. For the ETF, this reduces the likelihood of balance sheet deterioration due to excess credit, which is positive, but it also limits NII growth by volume, leaving stock performance more dependent on business margins and the cost of acquiring bank deposits.

The next pair of images we have show unrealized gains on investments and the number of banks on the list of problem banks. The first metric shows us that there are still losses in banks' securities portfolios, although these have fallen significantly in recent times. This variable is directly related to the evolution of long-term yields. If these improve, losses decrease. As I am not very clear about how they will evolve in the medium term due to the fiscal and inflationary uncertainty that exists, I am maintaining my hold rating. Finally, the number of banks on the list of “problem banks” remains close to historic lows, far from dynamics where a systemic crisis could be anticipated. This does not eliminate idiosyncratic risks, but it does support the idea that, at the aggregate level, the sector is in normalization mode after what happened in 2023.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Artiva Biotherapeutics: Upcoming Data Presentations May Bring Them Out Of Being Ignored
data.description:
Topline Summary

Artiva Biotherapeutics (ARTV) is one of a smattering of companies focused on cell therapies for the treatment of autoimmune diseases. In a world where the new hotness is engineered cell therapies to downregulate B cell activity (and therefore attenuate severe inflammatory conditions), ARTV is focusing on natural killer (NK) cells with no specific modifications to try and achieve deep B cell depletion. Today, I want to take a look at this company sitting at a rather rough valuation, and give my rationale for a tentative "Buy" sentiment.

Pipeline Overview

AlloNK (AB-101)

AlloNK is a cryopreserved, off-the-shelf cell therapy product that is unmodified (contrasting with some other cell therapy players engineering their cells with constructs like chimeric antigen receptors). NK cells act as sentinels against infections, possessing innate abilities to recognize antigens expressed on pathogens and activate an initial immune response. They are also thought to play important roles in the pathogenesis of autoimmune disease and cancer.

With respect to their main target of autoimmune diseases, ARTV is building on the observation that targeting B cells with antibodies like rituximab, in addition to providing these allogeneic NK cells, can induce deep B cell depletion, but in a manner that would not require high doses of lymphodepleting chemotherapy, thus rendering it a potentially safe procedure for the outpatient setting. Not to mention the need to involve oncology for follow-up due to the risk of cancers that have been observed with chimeric antigen receptor-based therapies.

ARTV has recently announced that they've selected refractory rheumatoid arthritis as their lead indication for AlloNK, for which they have FDA Fast Track designation. They are hopeful that conversations with the FDA in the first half of 2026 will yield a path toward initiating a pivotal trial for AlloNK in these patients.

Recently, we did get a first look at the initial safety and efficacy data for AlloNK in autoimmune disease, showcasing favorable tolerability and an ability to induce deep B-cell depletion when given in combination with a CD20 antibody. There were no instances of cytokine release syndrome or ICANS, both of which have dogged T cell-engaging treatments in oncology. No clinical findings were presented, but ARTV did disclose that B cells were depleted to unquantifiable levels by day 13 following treatment. So time will tell if this actually induces remission of these inflammatory conditions.

A specific cohort of patients with refractory rheumatoid arthritis will be the focus of a planned data readout sometime in the first half of 2026. The company has also guided that they intend to meet with the FDA to achieve alignment on a pivotal trial design for AlloNK in refractory rheumatoid arthritis.

At this year's Tandem Meetings, ARTV did present a study of AlloNK with or without rituximab in patients with non-Hodgkin lymphoma. Aside from cytopenias, there were few complications of note, as well as minimal cytokine release syndrome. Among 14 patients with no prior exposure to CAR T-cell therapy, 9 had a complete response to treatment, with 6 of those persisting for 12 months.

Taken together, while ARTV has stepped more in favor of autoimmune disease, this study does provide some level of proof of concept for the AlloNK platform, insomuch as supporting an ability to deplete B cells (non-Hodgkin lymphoma itself being a cancer of B cells, in fact).

Financial Overview

At the end of the last quarter, ARTV had $25.5 million in cash and equivalents, as well as $97.5 million in short-term investments. Operating expenses were $22.8 million, and after interest, the net loss was $21.5 million.

Cash burn for the first 9 months of 2025 was $62 million. At this rate, ARTV has approximately 6 quarters of liquidity to fund operations. This is consistent with management guidance that their cash runway should take them "into Q2 2027."

Strengths and Risks

Strength - Some evidence of enabling B cell depletion in a large target market

We have not seen much data that describes the use of ARTV's allogeneic NK cells in their lead indication, but the poster at Tandem gives an important clue that the product is doing something. It seemed to do much better when rituximab was added (64% response rate in CAR T-naive patients; 45% in all patients) than when given alone (25% limited complete response rate), and both approaches appeared to be rather feasible, with manageable toxicity.

In principle, being able to induce deep B-cell depletion even in settings of B-cell malignancy would suggest that the product is going to work to deplete B cells in the setting of autoimmune disease, as well. We have yet to see definitively if that's the case, but we have a signal that it could work well.

Risk - Funds running low

That said, ARTV is pinning a lot on their upcoming data updates in autoimmune disease, as their current market cap (hovering around $100 million as I write this) would not serve as the basis or collateral for an equity or debt raise, in my opinion. Therefore, they need this readout to set them into a position of strength to get funding, or else they'll be severely limited in 2026 when they want to spin up a pivotal trial.

Risk - One in a pack of cell therapy companies in autoimmune disease

It's also worth noting that ARTV is far from the only cell therapy player in the running to get into autoimmune disease. Companies like Autolus Therapeutics (AUTL), Cabaletta Bio (CABA), and Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY) are among the contenders in the space using engineered cell therapies. Of course, engineered cell therapies have their own substantial limitations, including manufacturing challenges, the need for lymphodepletion, and the ever-looming fears of serious complications like high-grade cytokine release syndrome, ICANS, or T-cell malignancy.

So by no means is ARTV out of the running just because there are other contenders, but I do not see clear indicators that AlloNK is particularly differentiated. Updates in the coming months and years are going to be critical to follow to see who might shake out.

Bottom-Line Summary

ARTV presents an interesting short-term play. Given their latest liquidity of almost $140 million, the market cap of around $100 million suggests that little to no value is attributed to their pipeline. This gives substantial near-term upside potential if their upcoming data readouts give a strong positive signal that AlloNK can help patients with autoimmune disease. Rheumatoid arthritis on its own is a market sized above $25 billion per year, after all.

Unfortunately, the totality of evidence we've seen to date has been in a malignancy that is related to autoimmune disease only in principle. It's important to keep in mind that cancer management has different tolerances for things like infection risk and other kinds of toxicity. There is plenty of reason to believe that the initial evidence of activity in non-Hodgkin lymphoma will not translate to rheumatoid arthritis. Time will tell. A lot is riding on these data coming soon, as well, as they're the best chance for ARTV to be placed in a position of strength to raise funds.

At this time, I am inclined to give a very tentative "Buy" sentiment to the ARTV investment thesis, based on the clear lack of appreciation for the potential of their pipeline by the market. Of course, one should be very cautious in entering into a position, given the number of things that can go wrong from a clinical data and financial perspective. Moreover, the relatively low volume of this equity might make it difficult to easily enter and exit a position.

Caveat emptor, but I see an opportunity for a significant correction in valuation to the upside.

data.title: EWA: Potentially Range Bound, Given The Mix Of Tailwinds And Headwinds
data.description:
Respectable Performance, But Underperforming Its Peers

The iShares MSCI Australia ETF (EWA) which we’ve previously covered over the years, hasn’t been a hugely lucrative bet for investors over the past year. To be fair, EWA, which currently offers access to 46 Australian stocks (98% of which are giant and large-caps), certainly hasn’t been a disappointment, with returns of around 14% over a 1-year time frame, but those returns are not a patch on what its developed market peers have managed (over 40%), or even global markets (27%) for that matter.

Could 2026 prove to be even more rewarding for EWA? Well, let’s examine some of the major themes that could drive sentiment towards and away from EWA.

How Are EWA’s Key Sectors Positioned?

EWA covers stocks from 11 different sectors, but interestingly just two sectors alone, (financials with a 41% stake), and materials (22%), jointly account for almost two-thirds of the portfolio.

The clout of the top sector- financials is reiterated even further by the fact that 4 out of EWA’s 5 top stocks all consists of banks, or those more popularly known in Australia as the D-SIB [Domestic Systematically Important Banks] cohort.

The outlook for this bunch going forward, looks quite mixed. For starters, investors should consider that inflation in Australia at 3.4%, is above the comfort zone of the central bank’s target of 2-3%. While it was encouraging to see the central bank cut its cash rate from levels of 4.35% at the start of 2025 to 3.6% by August, those rates haven’t been curtailed any further because inflation is still at a high threshold; in fact commentary earlier this month, from members of the RBA suggest that the trajectory going forward is likely to be that of further rate hikes rather than cuts. Basically, the Swap market is suggesting that in 2026 rates are set to be tightened by around 30bps in total. The prospect of higher policy rates will naturally leave an inimical mark on the appetite of bank credit, which in fairness had been progressing quite well over the months and has crossed the 7% YoY mark.

With the likelihood of a less favorable interest rate environment, Australian consumers are now spooked, with the forward-looking Consumer Sentiment Index on a downward spiral for two straight months. The report suggested that much of the trepidation around sentiment appears to be centered around higher interest rate expectations. Fitch Ratings has also suggested that the pace of loan growth in 2026 will likely slow to levels of 5%.

Looking at the plus side, if loan growth appetite isn’t expected to be as good as FY25, then these Australian banks will likely divert some of their excess capital to shareholder returns. Investors may be enthused to note that these DSIBs, all have solid capital ratios, with their respective CET1 ratios exceeding the minimum regulatory of 10.25% (in Australia, different categories of banks have to maintain different capital ratios).

Potentially strong dividend payouts by these banks in 2026, will be music to the ears of EWA investors who have come to rely on this product’s quarterly distributions for many years now. It’s worth noting that at current prices, EWA’s quarterly dividends annualize to an impressive 3.18% at current prices, which is better than the median dividend yield of all ETFs (2.78%).

Meanwhile, the Australian material sector too is staring at another robust year, with export volumes (both resources and energy) all set to hit record levels by 2027, driven by the strong global appetite for iron ore and gold.

Also consider that EWA’s second largest holding, BHP Group Limited (BHP), which is also the world’s largest copper producer, is well placed to leverage the ever-growing structural deficit in the market, which is expected to come in at hundreds of thousands of pounds in 2026.

How Is EWA Positioned to Developed Markets?

Prima facie, mean reversion chasing investors, who move funds around different developed market pockets, may be enthused to see that EWA’s current relative-strength [RS] ratio vs other developed markets looks very oversold, and could be due for a bounce. To elaborate, for close to two decades, the RS has averaged a figure of 0.57x, but currently it is at record lows of less than 0.41, which represents almost a 30% discount!

But for this mean reversion opportunity to play out and gain traction, one would also need to see EWA’s valuations come across as very cheap relative to its developed market peers; unfortunately, that is not the case at this juncture. EWA is priced at an earnings multiple of nearly 19x and a book value multiple of nearly 2.5x; this translates to earnings multiple premia of 28% and a book value multiple premia of 34% over the corresponding multiples of developed markets. We acknowledge that some investors may be open to paying top dollar, if the growth potential of a product was resilient enough, but that is questionable in EWA’s case. To highlight, EWA’s holdings are only poised to deliver long-term earnings of 6% (6% earnings on a 19x multiple implies a costly PEG ratio of over 3x), which lags developed market earnings growth by 260 bps!

Closing Thoughts- What Do The Technicals Suggest?

EWA’s long- and short-term charts suggest there is no great incentive in loading up on the ETF now, and neutral positioning would be more favorable.

On EWA’s long-term chart (the monthly), we can see that for close to 15 years now, the $27-$29 levels have served as a barrier with additional supply of shares coming in. At $26.54, EWA is hardly a breadth away from hitting those levels and is not anywhere close to its upward-sloping support, implying unfavorable risk-reward for a long position.

Separately also take note of developments on the shorter time frame chart- the daily chart—where all the key moving averages (50, 100, and 200) appear to be congregating rather than expanding (reflecting the lack of momentum). Further sideways action is reiterated by the ADX indicator at the lower end of the chart, which is now at less than 10 (for the uninitiated, readings below 20, point to a range-bound scenario)

To conclude, we are reiterating our hold on EWA.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Rates Spark: Sterling Rates And U.S. TIC Data
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker, Senior European Rates Strategist and Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas

Sterling rates reflecting growth optimism, but trend still lower

Whilst sterling rates have come down significantly over the past month, a rethink of the economic outlook could offset some of these gains. We still see further weakness in the jobs market, but Thursday’s monthly GDP numbers did bring some optimism.

When you see 5Y rates underperform the rest of the curve, markets are signalling a more positive take on the business cycle. In effect, this also pushes back the Bank of England's easing expectations. And indeed, the probability of a rate cut in March is gradually falling, with now just 9bp priced in.

Next week will be important for GBP rates as we get more inflation and jobs data. We may get a hawkish aftertaste if services inflation rises from a still hot 4.4% to 4.6% and the unemployment rate nudges lower.

In that case, markets will likely move the expectation of a March rate cut to April. The reaction should not, however, change the broader picture of inflation trending lower and growth still muted. By the end of this year, we therefore still think the 10Y gilt yield has room to move lower.

Latest US TIC data show that net buying of securities for November

US Treasury International Capital (TIC) data show robust inflows into the US for November. The total net inflows were $220bn. Of this overall net flow (which includes banking flows), bond flows show that foreigners bought a net $112bn in November. Canada, Norway, and Saudi Arabia were the biggest buyers, followed by the custodial centres. The biggest net sellers were the UAE, China, Germany, India, Mexico and Thailand.

For 2025 YTD, the biggest buyers were the UK, Japan and Canada, while the biggest sellers included China, India and Brazil. In total, net bond buying by foreigners cumulated to $740bn from January 2025 to November 2025.

Big picture, Japan remains the biggest foreign holder of Treasuries, at about $1.2tr, followed by the UK at a tad under $890bn and China at just under $690bn. The wider picture shows China as an ongoing large net seller, liquidating a net $350bn since 2022.

Overall, foreigners hold 33% of US Treasuries, with US domestics holding 52% (the Federal Reserve holds the rest).

Friday’s events and market views

No notable data from Europe. From the US, we will be looking at industrial production and capacity utilisation data for December. The Fed's Bowman and Jefferson will speak about the economic outlook, which will be the last external communication before the Fed blackout period.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post
